title: Herr Korbes
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 41. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The forty-first tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 41), a short, absurd chain-tale of deadpan cruelty. A little hen and little cock build a carriage and drive off, gathering up a cat, a millstone, an egg, a duck, a pin and a needle; at Herr Korbes’s empty house each hides, and when he comes home they ambush him one after another until the millstone crushes him dead — with no reason ever given but the narrator’s dry verdict that he must have been a wicked man. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as rothe, daß, gieng, gerieth, wüthend, Hausthür, todt and muß.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein Hühnchen und ein Hähnchen, die wollten zusammen eine Reise machen.
sentences.l: There was once a little hen and a little cock, who wanted to make a journey together.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there were a little hen and a little cock who wanted to take a journey together.
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ Hühnchen and Hähnchen are diminutives of Huhn (hen) and Hahn (cock) — the -chen ending, always neuter, gives a small, fond note you will meet all through Grimm.

sentences.t: Da baute das Hähnchen einen schönen Wagen, der vier rothe Räder hatte, und spannte vier Mäuschen davor.
sentences.l: Then the little cock built a fine carriage that had four red wheels, and harnessed four little mice before it.
sentences.i: So the little cock built a fine carriage with four red wheels, and harnessed four little mice in front of it.
sentences.n: “rothe” is the 1857 spelling of rote (‘red’); the th for t recurs throughout this edition (also Thüre, todt below). “spannte … davor” — davor here means ‘in front of it,’ the mice hitched before the carriage.

sentences.t: Das Hühnchen setzte sich mit dem Hähnchen auf und sie fuhren mit einander fort.
sentences.l: The little hen seated itself up with the little cock, and they drove off with one another.
sentences.i: The little hen climbed up with the little cock, and off they drove together.
sentences.n: Two separable verbs: “setzte sich … auf” (sich aufsetzen, to seat oneself up on) and “fuhren … fort” (fortfahren, to drive off) — the prefix jumps to the end of the clause, as German separables constantly do.

sentences.t: Nicht lange, so begegnete ihnen eine Katze, die sprach „wo wollt ihr hin?“
sentences.l: Not long, and there met them a cat, which spoke: ‘where do you want to go?’
sentences.i: Before long a cat met them and said, ‘Where are you off to?’
sentences.n: “begegnete ihnen” — begegnen takes the dative: it met *to them.* “wo wollt ihr hin?” drops the verb of motion (gehen/fahren): literally ‘where do you want to?’, i.e. ‘where are you going?’

sentences.t:
„als hinaus
nach des Herrn Korbes seinem Haus.“
sentences.l:
‘why, off out
to Sir Korbes his house.’
sentences.i:
‘Off and away
to Herr Korbes’s house.’
sentences.n: “des Herrn Korbes seinem Haus” is the folk possessive — the person is marked twice, once with the genitive (des Herrn Korbes) and again with a dative possessive (seinem Haus): ‘to Herr Korbes his house’ = ‘to Herr Korbes’s house.’ “als hinaus” is a colloquial ‘why, out …’ that launches the rhyme (Haus rhymes with hinaus).
“Nehmt … mit” is the separable verb mitnehmen (‘to take along’) in the plural/polite imperative — the cat addresses both birds.

sentences.t: Hähnchen antwortete „recht gerne, setz dich hinten auf, daß du vornen nicht herabfällst.
sentences.l: The little cock answered: ‘right gladly, seat yourself on behind, so that you do not fall down off the front.
sentences.i: The little cock answered, ‘Gladly; sit yourself down at the back, so you don’t tumble off the front.
sentences.n: “setz dich … auf” is again sich aufsetzen (‘seat yourself up’), imperative. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass. “vornen” is an older, dialectal form of vorn (‘at the front’).

sentences.t:
Nehmt euch wohl in acht
daß ihr meine rothen Räderchen nicht schmutzig macht.
Ihr Räderchen, schweift,
ihr Mäuschen, pfeift,
als hinaus
nach des Herrn Korbes seinem Haus.“
sentences.l:
Take good care of yourselves,
that you do not make my little red wheels dirty.
You little wheels, sweep round,
you little mice, whistle,
why, off out
to Sir Korbes his house.’
sentences.i:
Mind yourselves well,
that you don’t muddy my little red wheels.
You little wheels, spin round,
you little mice, pipe away,
off and away
to Herr Korbes’s house.’
sentences.n: “Nehmt euch … in acht” is sich in Acht nehmen, ‘to be on one’s guard, take care.’ The verse rhymes in pairs (acht/macht, schweift/pfeift, hinaus/Haus); “schweift” (schweifen, to sweep, roam) and “pfeift” (pfeifen, to whistle) are commands to the wheels and mice. Räderchen is the diminutive of Rad.

sentences.t: Danach kam ein Mühlstein, dann ein Ei, dann eine Ente, dann eine Stecknadel, und zuletzt eine Nähnadel, die setzten sich auch alle auf den Wagen und fuhren mit.
sentences.l: After that came a millstone, then an egg, then a duck, then a pin, and last a sewing needle; these all seated themselves too on the carriage and rode along.
sentences.i: After that came a millstone, then an egg, then a duck, then a pin, and last of all a sewing needle; and these all climbed onto the carriage too and rode along.
sentences.n: The travelling company grows by absurd accumulation — millstone, egg, duck, pin, needle — and each will later pay Herr Korbes back in its own way. “fuhren mit” is mitfahren (‘to ride along’), separable.

sentences.t: Wie sie aber zu des Herrn Korbes Haus kamen, so war der Herr Korbes nicht da.
sentences.l: But when they came to Sir Korbes’s house, Sir Korbes was not there.
sentences.i: But when they reached Herr Korbes’s house, Herr Korbes was not at home.
sentences.n: “Wie … kamen” — here wie means ‘when, as’ (not ‘how’). “da” here = ‘there, present’: he was not in.

sentences.t: Die Mäuschen fuhren den Wagen in die Scheune, das Hühnchen flog mit dem Hähnchen auf eine Stange, die Katze setzte sich ins Kamin, die Ente in die Bornstange, das Ei wickelte sich ins Handtuch, die Stecknadel steckte sich ins Stuhlkissen, die Nähnadel sprang aufs Bett mitten ins Kopfkissen, und der Mühlstein legte sich über die Thüre.
sentences.l: The little mice drove the carriage into the barn, the little hen flew with the little cock onto a pole, the cat sat down in the hearth, the duck onto the well-pole, the egg wrapped itself in the towel, the pin stuck itself in the chair-cushion, the sewing needle sprang onto the bed right into the pillow, and the millstone laid itself over the door.
sentences.i: The mice drove the carriage into the barn, the hen flew up with the cock onto a perch, the cat settled into the hearth, the duck onto the well-pole, the egg wrapped itself in the towel, the pin tucked itself into the chair-cushion, the needle leapt onto the bed right into the middle of the pillow, and the millstone laid itself above the door.
sentences.n: Each traveller hides exactly where it can ambush the master — the chain sets up the revenge sequence in order. “Kamin” here is the open hearth or fireplace; “Bornstange” (Born, an old word for well or spring, + Stange) is the pole over a well used to draw water. “Thüre” keeps the 1857 th-spelling (modern Tür).

sentences.t: Da kam der Herr Korbes nach Haus, gieng ans Kamin und wollte Feuer anmachen, da warf ihm die Katze das Gesicht voll Asche.
sentences.l: Then Sir Korbes came home, went to the hearth and wanted to make a fire; then the cat threw his face full of ashes.
sentences.i: Then Herr Korbes came home, went to the hearth and was about to light a fire, when the cat flung ashes all over his face.
sentences.n: The assault begins — and the joke of the tale is that no reason is ever given: Korbes has done nothing to any of them. “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); gieng, fieng, anfieng recur throughout the edition. “anmachen” (Feuer anmachen, to light a fire) is separable.

sentences.t: Er lief geschwind in die Küche und wollte sich abwaschen, da sprützte ihm die Ente Wasser ins Gesicht.
sentences.l: He ran quickly into the kitchen and wanted to wash himself, then the duck squirted water into his face.
sentences.i: He ran quickly into the kitchen to wash himself, when the duck squirted water in his face.
sentences.n: “sprützte” is the 1857 spelling of spritzte (‘squirted, splashed’). “ihm … ins Gesicht” — the dative ihm marks whose face: ‘into his face.’

sentences.t: Er wollte sich an dem Handtuch abtrocknen, aber das Ei rollte ihm entgegen, zerbrach und klebte ihm die Augen zu.
sentences.l: He wanted to dry himself on the towel, but the egg rolled toward him, broke, and glued his eyes shut.
sentences.i: He tried to dry himself on the towel, but the egg rolled toward him, broke, and sealed his eyes shut.
sentences.n: “rollte ihm entgegen” — entgegen (‘toward’) takes the dative ihm. “klebte … zu” is zukleben (‘to paste, glue shut’), separable — the prefix zu lands at the end.

sentences.t: Er wollte sich ruhen, und setzte sich auf den Stuhl, da stach ihn die Stecknadel.
sentences.l: He wanted to rest, and sat himself down on the chair, then the pin stuck him.
sentences.i: He wanted to rest and sat down on the chair, when the pin pricked him.
sentences.n: “stach” is the preterite of stechen (‘to prick, sting’).

sentences.t: Er gerieth in Zorn, und warf sich aufs Bett, wie er aber den Kopf aufs Kissen niederlegte, stach ihn die Nähnadel, so daß er aufschrie und ganz wüthend in die weite Welt laufen wollte.
sentences.l: He fell into a rage, and threw himself on the bed; but as he laid his head down on the cushion, the sewing needle stuck him, so that he cried out and, quite furious, wanted to run off into the wide world.
sentences.i: He flew into a rage and threw himself on the bed; but as he laid his head down on the pillow, the needle pricked him, so that he cried out and, quite beside himself, wanted to run off into the wide world.
sentences.n: “gerieth” is the 1857 form of geriet (from geraten): in Zorn geraten = ‘to fall into a rage.’ “niederlegte” is niederlegen (‘to lay down,’ separable) and “aufschrie” is aufschreien (‘to cry out,’ separable). “wüthend” is the 1857 spelling of wütend (‘furious’).

sentences.t: Wie er aber an die Hausthür kam, sprang der Mühlstein herunter und schlug ihn todt.
sentences.l: But when he came to the house-door, the millstone sprang down and struck him dead.
sentences.i: But when he reached the house-door, the millstone leapt down and struck him dead.
sentences.n: “Hausthür” and “todt” keep the 1857 th-spelling (modern Haustür, tot). “schlug ihn todt” — totschlagen (‘to strike dead’), here split around the object.

sentences.t: Der Herr Korbes muß ein recht böser Mann gewesen sein.
sentences.l: Sir Korbes must have been a really wicked man.
sentences.i: Herr Korbes must have been a very wicked man indeed.
sentences.n: The tale’s deadpan close: after a killing for which no motive is ever offered, the narrator supplies the ‘moral’ backwards — he *must* have been wicked, since he was so punished. “muß” is the 1857 spelling of muss.
